children.text: Eightieth session – Agenda item 150 – Financing of the United Nations Interim Security Force for Abyei – Budget performance for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and budget for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 for the United Nations Interim Security Force for Abyei – Report of the Secretary-General

The present report contains the budget performance and the budget for the United Nations Interim Security Force for Abyei (UNISFA) for the periods from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027, respectively.

During the reporting period, UNISFA continued to implement key elements of its mandate, driven by the following strategic priorities: (a) protection of civilians and facilitation of humanitarian assistance; (b) support for the political process, including support for Joint Border Verification Monitoring Mechanism activities; (c) promotion of coexistence between communities through dialogue and mediation; (d) peacebuilding activities such as the Joint Programme of the Resident Coordinators in the Sudan and South Sudan in Abyei; and (e) promotion of accountability, access to justice and support for the rule of law. UNISFA conducted more than 40,000 patrols and supported intercommunal dialogues, including a youth-led peace initiative between Ngok and Twic Dinka communities, resulting in a peace framework. UNISFA also established a Joint Women’s Committee to address women’s issues, with women’s participation in peace processes increasing to 41 per cent. UNISFA programmatic activities benefited more than 70,000 residents, enhancing healthcare and livelihoods, while maintaining a zero-tolerance policy on sexual exploitation. Training strengthened law enforcement and human rights, while community legal aid helped reduce detention. Operational improvements included data-driven tools for decision-making, collectively strengthening stability and peacebuilding efforts in Abyei.

During the 2026/27 period, despite the challenging circumstances under which UNISFA continues to operate, exacerbated by the ongoing conflict in the Sudan and political instability in South Sudan, UNISFA will continue to focus on the strategic priorities outlined above. UNISFA will also: (a) tackle operational difficulties such as the closure of airspace by evaluating logistical efficiencies and through enhanced collaboration with the United Nations Mission in South Sudan, especially with the expansion of the Force’s footprint in Wau owing to the need to reduce operations in Kadugli; and (b) attempt to continue critical projects which, owing to the need to reprioritize resources, could not be completed in prior periods and face continued delays in the current period, such as camp reconstruction and replacement of assets, especially under ground transportation, that are past their operational life, to ensure that UNISFA has the necessary capability to ensure safe, sustainable accommodations and support services for all its personnel, including camp reconstruction and asset replacement. UNISFA will also continue to embrace innovative technology, such as enhancement of digital transformation and data analytics.

In the 2024/25 period, UNISFA incurred $296.8 million in expenditure for the budget performance period, representing a resource utilization rate of 99.7 per cent, compared with $287.1 million in expenditure and a resource utilization rate of 100.0 per cent in the 2023/24 period.

The unencumbered balance of $1.0 million reflects the net impact of: (a) increased requirements for military and police personnel, attributable primarily to a new higher-cost contract for rations (owing mainly to the conflict in the Sudan) and to higher actual rates for mission subsistence allowance, owing to higher actual rates for the Sudan, effective 1 January 2025; (b) reduced requirements for civilian personnel, attributable primarily to a higher actual average vacancy rate of 11.6 per cent, for international staff, compared with 9.6 per cent in the approved budget, owing to liquidity constraints; and (c) reduced requirements for operational costs, attributable primarily to the Force’s postponement (owing to liquidity constraints) of planned acquisitions of goods and materials, and to a lower actual number of flight hours, compared with budgeted estimates, owing to the cancellation of the contract for one fixed-wing aircraft and the unavailability of rotary-wing aircraft due to scheduled maintenance and unexpected technical issues.

The budget proposed for the 2026/27 period in the amount of $277.2 million represents a decrease of $31.9 million, or 10.3 per cent, compared with the apportionment of $309.1 million approved for the 2025/26 period.

The decrease for the 2026/27 period reflects reduced requirements under: (a) military and police personnel, attributable primarily to higher vacancy rates for military observers and military contingent personnel; (b) civilian personnel, attributable primarily to higher vacancy rates for international staff and general temporary assistance positions, national General Service staff and United Nations volunteers; and (c) operational costs, attributable primarily to lower costs for air operations, owing to the proposed reduction by one helicopter and to a reduced number of flight hours for helicopters, for diesel fuel, due primarily to lower average prices per litre, to fewer contracted communications and information technology personnel and for freight, due to an anticipated decreased number of acquisitions, lessening requirements for freight forwarding, inland transportation and customs clearance services.
(Thousands of United States dollars; budget year is from 1 July to 30 June)

The actions to be taken by the General Assembly are set out in section IV of the present report.

text: I.	Mandate, results achieved and planned outcome

children.text: A.	Mandate

1.	The mandate of the United Nations Interim Security Force for Abyei (UNISFA) was established by the Security Council in its resolution 1990 (2011) and extended in subsequent resolutions. The mandate for the performance period was provided by the Council in its resolutions 2708 (2023) and 2760 (2024). The most recent extension of the mandate was authorized by the Council in its resolution 2802 (2025), by which it extended the mandate until 15 November 2026.

2.	The Force is mandated to support the implementation of the Agreement between the Government of the Republic of the Sudan and the Sudan People’s Liberation Movement on Temporary Arrangements for the Administration and Security of the Abyei Area of 20 June 2011, by participating in relevant Abyei Area bodies, monitoring the demilitarized status of the Area, facilitating humanitarian access and liaison between the parties, protecting the Abyei Area from incursions by unauthorized elements and supporting the Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism in creating a safe demilitarized border zone.
B.	Budget implementation for 2024/25

3.	The five key priorities for UNISFA remained unchanged during the reporting period, and all the following actions contributed to the implementation of these priorities: (a) physical protection of civilians and facilitation of humanitarian assistance; (b) provision of support for the political process between the Sudan and South Sudan, in cooperation with relevant stakeholders, including provision of support for the monitoring and verification activities of the Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism along the safe demilitarized border zone to ensure that the Abyei Area is demilitarized and free of weapons; (c) local-level mediation to address intercommunal conflict; (d) conduct of peacebuilding activities to address the root causes of conflict, including through the Joint Programme of the Resident Coordinators in the Sudan and South Sudan in Abyei, with the United Nations country teams; and (e) promotion of accountability, access to justice and support for the rule of law.

4.	Discussions between the Parties on the status of Abyei made no progress during the reporting period owing to the continuation of hostilities in the Sudan and political dynamics in South Sudan, but UNISFA maintained regular engagement with the Sudanese and South Sudanese authorities with regard to Abyei and the safe demilitarized border zone. Despite the support expressed by the Parties to resume discussions through the Joint Political and Security Mechanism and the Abyei Joint Oversight Committee, reminders by UNISFA to both parties went unanswered.

5.	Within the Abyei Area, UNISFA forces continued to respond promptly to incidents, which resulted in no violent armed clashes being recorded and helped ensure the protection of civilians, including internally displaced persons and refugees. In addition to substantial day and night patrols, UNISFA provided force protection and escort operations to support the movement of United Nations agencies, uniformed personnel, delegates and humanitarian actors in the Abyei Area.

6.	UNISFA continued to support humanitarian efforts by maintaining area control and, upon request, offering armed protection to aid teams in the provision of humanitarian assistance in the field. It also assisted the movement of humanitarian personnel and supplies using its air assets along routes such as Abyei-Wau and Abyei-Kadugli and helped implement flood mitigation measures through engineering projects in order to keep access roads open and maintain the Agok airstrip and also provided water, sanitation and hygiene, including water and waste management, services at the refugee transit centre in Abyei.

7.	UNISFA also continued to support intercommunity dialogue initiatives, by facilitating weekly joint community peace committee meetings, pre- and post-migration conferences and, in partnership with the United Nations Mission in South Sudan (UNMISS), a youth-led dialogue in Wau, South Sudan, between the Ngok Dinka and Twic Dinka communities, substantially contributing to the maintenance of peace and protection of civilians in the Abyei Area.

8.	Despite supply chain disruptions, which impacted the deployment of both staff and supplies, as well as project timelines, UNISFA (in collaboration with United Nations country teams and implementing partners) continued the Joint Programme of the Resident Coordinators in the Sudan and South Sudan in Abyei in support of a range of humanitarian, development and peace projects in Abyei, to promote peacebuilding, enhance social services and mitigate conflict. The Programme prioritized the provision of community services, livelihoods, food security, law enforcement support and peacebuilding, in line with the Force’s mandate.

9.	To advance the participation of women in peace processes, UNISFA facilitated the inaugural joint Ngok Dinka and Misseriya Women’s Conference, in June 2025, which resulted in an agreement to establish a Joint Women’s Committee. In terms of gender mainstreaming, resources from the Force’s programmatic activities helped advance the women and peace and security mandate, with initiatives focused on women’s capacity-building, the prevention of sexual and gender-based violence and the engagement of women in peace processes. UNISFA also implemented, as part of its quick-impact projects programme, women and peace and security initiatives through infrastructure projects with the Abyei Women’s Union.

10.	With the continued absence of the Abyei Police Service, United Nations police continued to support, mentor and reinforce community protection committees and joint protection committees and also supported protection initiatives by overseeing investigations and through the sharing of information with civilian partners. UNISFA advanced its rule of law support strategy, in collaboration with United Nations country teams and local communities.

11.	Despite the crisis in the Sudan and the closure of its airspace, the Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism was granted free movement within the safe demilitarized border zone, but aerial monitoring missions have been on hold since April 2023. North of Kadugli, clashes persisted between the Sudanese Armed Forces and the Sudan People’s Liberation Movement-North (SPLM-N) and the Rapid Support Forces, respectively. Sudan People’s Liberation Movement-North control of the main supply route – as exemplified by the seizure by the Rapid Support Forces on 28 February 2025 of United Nations-contracted fuel trucks in West Kordofan State, in the Sudan – forced UNISFA to halt road resupply from Abyei to the Kadugli logistics base and to temporarily move the Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism headquarters from Kadugli to Abyei.

12.	The increased presence, south of the River Kiir, of the South Sudan People’s Defence Forces and the Sudan People’s Liberation Movement impeded the Force’s efforts to ensure that Abyei remains a weapons-free area. In addition, the incursion of Rapid Support Forces elements north of Amiet market and their presence along the Abyei-Kadugli road hindered and threatened operations of the Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism. United Nations agencies, funds and programmes were also unable to deploy north of Abyei town owing to the ongoing conflict, which contributed to delays in executing peacebuilding initiatives such as the Joint Programme. The large human influx into and through Abyei further stretched the Force’s humanitarian assistance facilitation efforts.

13.	Continued hostilities in the Sudan negatively impacted engagement between the Governments of the Sudan and South Sudan on the issue of Abyei during the reporting period. During engagements with both Parties, UNISFA continued to advocate for the convening of meetings of the Abyei Joint Oversight Committee and Joint Political and Security Mechanism, but to no avail. Despite continued engagement with Government of the Sudan authorities in Port Sudan, no progress was made on: (a) the establishment of the Abyei Police Service; (b) the deployment of formed police units and the full Security Council-authorized complement of individual police officers; (c) the appointment of a civilian Deputy Head of Mission; or (d) the operationalization of Athony Airstrip. Lastly, no progress has been made by the Parties on the re-establishment of operations at Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism Sector 1 in South Sudan.
C.	Planning assumptions and mission support initiatives for 2026/27

14.	In the light of the prevailing situation, UNISFA will continue to focus on its strategic priorities, including continued: (a) physical protection of civilians and facilitation of humanitarian assistance; (b) support for the political process between the Sudan and South Sudan, including the provision of support for Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism activities along the safe demilitarized border zone; (c) promotion of peaceful coexistence between communities through dialogue and local-level mediation; (d) peacebuilding activities, including through the Abyei Joint Programme; and (e) promotion of accountability, access to justice and support for the rule of law. In accordance with Security Council resolution 2802 (2025), by which the Council extended the Force’s mandate until 15 November 2026, UNISFA will prepare an additional report for the Council on security situation activities undertaken by authorities in the Sudan and South Sudan to assess demonstrable progress towards achieving benchmarks outlined by the Council, and it will provide a comprehensive assessment of the security implications of any potential drawdown.

15.	The goal is to implement the Force’s mandate by protecting civilians in the Abyei Area, addressing security challenges such as arms proliferation and intercommunal violence and supporting the Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism. UNISFA will continue to tackle operational difficulties in the Joint Mechanism area and to work towards resuming Mechanism meetings. The incident in February 2025 in which a logistical convoy was abducted and fuel trucks confiscated by the Rapid Support Forces significantly disrupted logistical supplies to the Joint Mechanism. The restrictions on carrying out mandated border monitoring operations are compounded by the ongoing conflict in the Sudan and the closure of the airspace. UNISFA will therefore evaluate logistical efficiencies and enhance its collaboration with UNMISS, especially with regard to UNISFA planned expansions in Wau, South Sudan.

16.	Efforts will be further focused on maintaining the peace dividends gained for the Ngok Dinka and Twic Dinka communities, including by managing the presence of armed youth from the Ngok Dinka, while monitoring the deployment of the South Sudan Security Forces and the presence of the Rapid Support Forces in the Abyei Area. UNISFA will facilitate intercommunal dialogues, including pre- and post-migration meetings and traditional leaders’ conferences, while promoting the inclusion of women and youth. UNISFA will continue to support the joint community peace committee as the primary mechanism for conflict and dispute management and resolution, including overseeing migration corridors. UNISFA will also accelerate implementation of the youth and peace and security agenda through dialogues, training and capacity-building initiatives.

17.	On the security front, recalibrating deployments of the Force to address new security concerns, particularly in areas impacted by intensified activity from the Rapid Support Forces, is crucial. UNISFA will harness its operational effectiveness through integrated patrols, tackle weapons proliferation and provide physical protection to the civilian population, including displaced persons. Strengthening early warning mechanisms and enhancing community engagement are essential for civilian protection, especially in the Amiet market area. The deployment of the full United Nations police capacity – in agreement with both the Sudanese and South Sudanese Governments – is also a priority, along with the confiscation and destruction of weapons in coordination with local authorities.

18.	The rule of law support strategy for Abyei will guide the implementation of the rule of law mandate, focused on containing criminal activities in the Amiet market area as part of the recommendations contained in the 2025 strategic review. It will enhance dispute resolution and strengthen community mechanisms such as: the joint community peace committee; the joint protection committee; joint traditional courts; and the community protection committee. This is to promote accountability and access to justice and law and order and improve detention practices, all while adhering to human rights standards. There will be a particular focus on addressing gender-based violence and integrating crime prevention and violence reduction strategies.

19.	Along with the Force’s community liaison work, the Abyei Joint Programme will continue to be the primary mechanism for promoting peace in Abyei by providing essential services such as healthcare, animal husbandry and nutrition assistance, especially for vulnerable members of the community. UNISFA will collaborate with participating United Nations agencies, funds and programmes, and with the Resident Coordinators’ Offices in the Sudan and South Sudan, to align with existing initiatives and ensure sustainable support. The Programme will also continue to focus on fostering social cohesion and peaceful coexistence through dialogue and local peacebuilding activities. Humanitarian assistance and peacebuilding in northern Abyei will remain a crucial tenet of the Programme.

20.	UNISFA will promote women’s equal and safe participation in peace processes in Abyei through local dispute resolution and community protection committees. This includes intercommunal dialogues, support for women’s civil society organizations and leadership capacity-building, with particular efforts to protect women’s rights and prevent sexual and gender-based violence, through sensitization sessions. Local response mechanisms, especially around the Amiet market area, which are aimed at enhancing referral pathways for survivors of violence and increasing women’s representation in military and police components to improve gender parity, will also be strengthened.

21.	UNISFA will enhance efforts to ensure accountability and compliance with the United Nations zero-tolerance policy against sexual exploitation and abuse. This includes timely training for UNISFA personnel and support during predeployment visits to troop-contributing countries. Dedicated uniformed focal points will address these issues within military units, complemented by regular visits and inspections at deployment sites. Victims will receive essential support, such as counselling, mental health services and vocational training, through various initiatives agreed under the protection from sexual exploitation and abuse network.

22.	Cross-cutting initiatives will align with Action for Peacekeeping Plus, focusing on gender parity, innovative technology in peacekeeping and countering misinformation and hate speech. UNISFA will also prioritize enhancing digital transformation and data analytics to fulfil its mandate more effectively. The focus will be on improving operational efficiency, promoting political engagement, ensuring the safety and security of personnel and implementing community-focused initiatives, all while taking into consideration financial constraints and operational challenges.
Mission support initiatives

23.	UNISFA remains committed to the reconstruction of military operating bases, incorporating the principles of the Secretary-General’s Action for Peacekeeping and Action for Peacekeeping Plus, and to minimizing the Force’s environmental footprint as an integral part of the broader United Nations environmental strategy.

24.	During the 2026/27 period, to underscore the above-mentioned commitment, UNISFA plans to continue its camp reconstruction effort, aimed at replacing outdated infrastructure and designed to improve the safety and well-being of peacekeepers and to overcome barriers for women in peacekeeping.

25.	The proposed budget for the 2026/27 period includes the following significant projects: (a) the establishment of Wau as a logistics base, owing to the closure of the northern main supply route and to strengthen supply chain resilience along the southern route; (b) the replacement of microwave links at all team sites; (c) the construction of lift station pumps at three team sites; (d) the provision of horizontal infrastructure construction services across Sectors Central, North and South; (e) the completion and reconstruction of camps in Goli, Marial Achak and Noong; (f) the installation of water pumps and appurtenances at three team sites; (g) the construction of two supply chain warehouses at UNISFA headquarters; and (h) upgrading the electrical distribution network.

26.	In the context of the Secretary-General’s UN80 Initiative, the proposed budget reflects targeted efficiency measures through organizational optimization, functional streamlining and more efficient resource allocation. These measures follow a comprehensive review of operational requirements, workload distribution and support functions and are implemented through organizational realignments and other optimization measures, functional streamlining and allocative efficiencies. Efficiencies are reflected in all budget groups and include construction, environment, ground transportation, air operations, communications, information technology, medical and mine action (as outlined in section II.C of the present report).

27.	The proposed efficiency measures are expected to result in a more streamlined organizational structure; however, flexibility and expediency in responding to peaks in workload or unanticipated operational demands would be reduced. While all core mandated activities will continue, certain tasks may be implemented with adjusted modalities or modified timelines. Similarly, reductions in major equipment are expected to have a minor operational impact, as resources are carefully aligned with essential activities and force posture requirements. These adjustments reflect the need to balance operational effectiveness with the reduced level of personnel.

28.	In that regard, in line with evolving priorities and with the aim of enabling UNISFA to maintain mandate-critical functions and ensure efficient allocation of resources, the mission proposes: (a) reduced deployment of military contingent personnel, military staff officers and military observers; (b) abolishment of posts and temporary positions, including some that are directly related to military deployment, in the Offices of the Police Commissioner and the Joint Border Verification Monitoring Mechanism; (c) reduction by one helicopter; and (d) closure of locations.

29.	To mitigate potential risks associated with these efficiency initiatives, UNISFA has strengthened internal coordination, management oversight and prioritization mechanisms. Resources remain focused on critical mandate-related tasks, and coordination between civilian and uniformed components has been reinforced to ensure continuity of operations. UNISFA will continue to monitor the impact of the efficiency measures and to take corrective action, as necessary and in line with evolving priorities, to ensure continued effective mandate delivery.
Civilian staffing review

30.	During the first half of the 2025/26 period, a civilian staffing review was conducted by UNISFA and the Department of Operational Support, in accordance with paragraph 28 of General Assembly resolution 76/274. The review included an assessment of workforce capabilities, upskilling and reskilling needs, gender and diversity considerations and the role of affiliated personnel. The review was strongly guided by the maximizing of efficiencies, and its recommendations therefore highlighted opportunities to repurpose resources through current and anticipated vacancies, the sharing of services by units within UNISFA and other efficiency measures. Overall, the changes endorsed in the review – most of which are reflected in the 2026/27 budget and can be implemented within existing resources – support the progressive nationalization of critical functions and the streamlining of support structures without affecting mandate delivery or operational effectiveness.

31.	In response to the findings in the review, UNISFA implemented targeted staffing adjustments and organizational realignments in order to improve efficiency and to strengthen support delivery and also reprofiled existing positions to address operational gaps.

32.	As part of this exercise, 17 national General Service posts are proposed to be established for 2026/27, to replace 37 general temporary assistance replacement positions. This is in response to the observation by the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions of “the upcoming civilian staffing review at UNISFA and considers that the review presents an opportunity for the Secretary-General to critically assess the national general temporary assistance replacement positions with a view to determining the need for their continuation and the overall optimization of the staffing structures at UNISFA” (A/79/724/Add.11, para. 20). Following the review, as noted above, it is proposed to replace 17 of the 37 positions with new national General Service posts and to eliminate provision for the remaining 20, with their functions being covered through internal redeployment, the reprofiling of existing posts and process re-engineering and optimization. This will reduce reliance on temporary staffing arrangements and enhance cost transparency and workforce stability while retaining essential capacity.
D.	Regional mission cooperation

33.	During the 2024/25 period, UNISFA collaborated with regional organizations, including the African Union and African Union High-Level Implementation Panel, as well as the Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD). Initiatives aimed at facilitating dialogue with the African Union and IGAD were hampered by external factors, in particular ongoing conflict in the Sudan and political instability in South Sudan, in addition to the non-functioning of mechanisms such as the Abyei Joint Oversight Committee. Nonetheless, coinciding with visits to Juba by UNISFA leadership, UNISFA engaged with representatives from the African Union Commission in Juba to discuss matters related to Abyei and collaborated with UNMISS in domains including information-sharing, Joint Programme implementation and logistical support. However, despite these collaborative efforts, significant challenges remain.

34.	During the 2026/27 period, UNISFA will continue to engage the Governments of the Sudan and South Sudan, the African Union and other stakeholders to support efforts aimed at reviving political progress for Abyei once conditions allow. These efforts include facilitating resumption of meetings of the Joint Political and Security Mechanism and the Abyei Joint Oversight Committee. UNISFA will also hold regular coordination and information-sharing meetings with UNMISS and the Office of the Special Envoy of the Secretary-General for the Horn of Africa to address issues of mutual concern, including the final status of Abyei, cross-border security and transhumance issues and will continue to work closely with the United Nations country teams in the Sudan and South Sudan to ensure that efforts are joint or complementary across all funding streams and programmes.
E.	Human resources
Executive direction and management
Component 1: security, governance and border monitoring
a	Funded under general temporary assistance
Component 2: support
a	Funded under general temporary assistance.
F.	Proposed staffing changes

35.	In accordance with the civilian staffing review (see para. 30), outlined in the table below, it is proposed to: (a) establish 1 international (P-3) post, 4 United Nations Volunteer positions (2 international and 2 national) and 17 national General Service posts (see para. 32); (b) the reassignment of 36 posts (1 P-5, 3 P-4, 5 P-3, 1 P-2, 5 Field Service, 1 National Professional Officer and 20 national General Service) and 2 international United Nations Volunteer positions; and (c) the redeployment of 34 posts (1 P-4, 3 P-3, 13 Field Service, 1 National Professional Officer and 16 national General Service) and 5 international United Nations Volunteer positions. In addition to the 20 general temporary assistance replacement positions mentioned in paragraph 32, it is also proposed to abolish 6 posts (l D-1, 2 P-5, 1 P-4, 1 P-3 and 1 Field Service) and 1 general temporary assistance position (P-3). These post action proposals are also the result of the following organizational changes.

36.	As recommended in the review, it is also proposed to implement a new two-pillar support structure, comprising the Operations and Resource Management Section and the Supply Chain and Service Delivery Services Section. To form the latter new section, it is proposed to merge the Supply Chain Management Section and Service Delivery Services Sections. To preserve segregation of duties and mitigate procurement-related risk exposure by avoiding conflicts of interest and enforcing internal control, the Procurement, Medical Services and Supply Chain Performance Units (the latter to be renamed the Property Management Unit) are proposed to be moved to the Operations and Resources Management Section. As part of this realignment, it is proposed to:
Abolish the post of Chief, Supply Chain Management (P-5).
37.	The Sector/Regional Offices unit in the Operations and Resource Management Section is proposed to be abolished. As part of this realignment, it is proposed to:

(a)	Reassign six posts – to the Office of the Chief of Staff (Civilian) (1 national General Service); Community Liaison Office (1 P-3); Office of the Chief of Mission Support (1 P-3); Operations and Resource Management Section (1 P-3); Medical Services Unit (1 national General Service) and Movement Control and Distribution Unit (1 national General Service) – and redeploy one post to the Conduct and Discipline Section (national General Service);

(b)	Abolish one general temporary assistance position of Logistics Officer (P-3), which was established as from 1 July 2020 to provide logistical coordination for Joint Border Verification Monitoring Mechanism activities.

38.	In accordance with the recommendations contained in the review, to improve and consolidate essential language support services in support of mandate community-facing activities carried out by civilian, military and police components, it is proposed to establish a separate Translation Unit under the Office of the Chief of Staff (Civilian), through:
(a)	The establishment of 15 posts of Field Language Assistant (national General Service);
(b)	The redeployment of three posts of Field Language Assistant (national General Service) (two from the Office of the Police Commissioner and one from the Office of the Head of Mission);
(c)	The reassignment of one post of Political Affairs Officer (P-3), in the Joint Operations Centre/Joint Mission Analysis Centre, to a post of Field Translator (P-3) in the proposed new unit.

39.	In order to strengthen oversight and accountability and compliance with global United Nations facilities management and environmental standards, it is also proposed to establish a Facilities Management Unit under the new Supply Chain and Service Delivery Services Section, through:

(a)	The redeployment of 23 posts (1 P-4, 1 P-3, 9 Field Service, 1 National Professional Officer and 11 national General Service) and 5 international United Nations Volunteer positions from the Engineering Unit and 1 post (Field Service) from the Field Technology Services Unit to the proposed new unit;

(b)	The reassignment of two posts of Facilities Management Assistant (national General Service) from the Engineering Unit, as Water and Sanitation Assistant posts in the proposed new Facilities Management Unit.

40.	Also in accordance with recommendations contained in the review, it is proposed: (a) to move the Passenger Booking Unit, which is currently a separate unit under the Service Delivery Services Section, to within the Movement Control and Distribution Unit; and (b) to redeploy the staff of the Passenger Booking Unit (1 Field Service and 1 national General Service) from the former Service Delivery Services section to the Movement Control and Distribution Unit.

41.	In the context of efficiency and the Secretary-General’s UN80 initiative, it is proposed to abolish the Audit Response Unit, under the Office of the Chief of Mission Support, since the Unit’s functions can be carried out within the existing resources of the Office. Accordingly, it is proposed to:
(a)	Abolish one post of Auditor (P-4);
(b)	Redeploy one post of Claims Assistant (Field Service) to the Office of the Chief of Mission Support.
42.	Similarly, it is also proposed to abolish four other substantive posts:
(a)	One post of Chief of Service, Rule of Law (D-1) and one post of Administrative Assistant (Field Service) in the Office of the Chief, Joint Border Verification Monitoring Mechanism;
(b)	One post of Deputy Police Commissioner (P-5) in the Office of the Police Commissioner;
(c)	One post of Protocol Assistant (Field Service) in the Office of Chief of Staff.
43.	In support of the Force’s mine action programme objectives, it is proposed to continue:
(a)	Seven general temporary assistance positions (1 P-4, 1 P-3, 1 P-2, 3 Field Service and 1 national General Service) in the Mine Action Section under the Office of the Head of Mission;

(b)	Two P-3 general temporary assistance positions (1 Logistics Officer in the Mission Support Centre and 1 Transport Officer in the Transport Unit), established as from 1 July 2024 as part of the transition of the delivery model for mine action.

44.	In addition, it is proposed to continue one general temporary assistance position of Engineer (P-3), established as from 1 July 2013 in the Engineering and Facilities Management Unit, to oversee ongoing Force mobility projects.

children.text: G.	Results-based budgeting frameworks
Component 1: security, governance and border monitoring

table_content.text: 1.1	Provision of a safe and secure environment that facilitates peaceful migration and enables the delivery of humanitarian aid; and strengthened capability of the Abyei Police Service, in accordance with the 20 June 2011 Agreement
1.1.1	Zero incidents of attacks against humanitarian actors (2022/23: 0; 2023/24: 1; 2024/25: 0; 2025/26: 0; 2026/27: 0)

1.1	Provision of a safe and secure environment that facilitates peaceful migration and enables the delivery of humanitarian aid; and strengthened capability of the Abyei Police Service, in accordance with the 20 June 2011 Agreement
1.1.2	Zero incidents of intercommunal violence during migration (2022/23: 0; 2023/24: 0; 2024/25: 0; 2025/26: 0; 2026/27: 0)

1.1	Provision of a safe and secure environment that facilitates peaceful migration and enables the delivery of humanitarian aid; and strengthened capability of the Abyei Police Service, in accordance with the 20 June 2011 Agreement

1.1.3	Abyei Area largely free of armed personnel, assets and weapons, except those of UNISFA (number of occasions when the presence of armed personnel, assets and weapons was recorded (2022/23: 136; 2023/24: 100; 2024/25: 74; 2025/26: 30; 2026/27: 0)

1.1	Provision of a safe and secure environment that facilitates peaceful migration and enables the delivery of humanitarian aid; and strengthened capability of the Abyei Police Service, in accordance with the 20 June 2011 Agreement

1.1.4	Zero movements by UNISFA, the Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism, humanitarians or civilians interrupted or cancelled as a result of mines or explosive remnants of war within the Abyei Area and the Safe Demilitarized Border Zone (2022/23: 0; 2023/24: 0; 2024/25: 0; 2025/26: 0; 2026/27: 0)

1.1	Provision of a safe and secure environment that facilitates peaceful migration and enables the delivery of humanitarian aid; and strengthened capability of the Abyei Police Service, in accordance with the 20 June 2011 Agreement

1.1.5	Free movement of humanitarian agents within the area because of enhanced protection of civilians and information-sharing in the northern part of the Abyei Area (2022/23: 100 per cent; 2023/24: 100 per cent; 2024/25: 100 per cent; 2025/26: 100 per cent; 2026/27: 100 per cent)
1.2	Effective functioning of the Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism

1.2.1	Withdrawal of forces within the Safe Demilitarized Border Zone confirmed and allegations verified through the monitoring of 100 per cent of the uncontested Safe Demilitarized Border Zone (2022/23: 100 per cent; 2023/24: 60 per cent; 2024/25: 60 per cent; 2025/26: 100 per cent; 2026/27: 100 per cent)
1.2	Effective functioning of the Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism

1.2.2	Full deployment of Mine Action Section personnel to the Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism headquarters, all active sector headquarters and active team sites to ensure support for the Mechanism as required (2022/23: 100 per cent; 2023/24: 100 per cent; 2024/25: 100 per cent; 2025/26: 100 per cent; 2026/27: 100 per cent)
1.2	Effective functioning of the Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism

1.2.3	Full deployment of all national monitors from the Sudan and South Sudan to participate in Joint Border Verification and Monitoring Mechanism operational activities at Mechanism headquarters, Sector 1 headquarters, Sector 2 headquarters and all team sites (2023/24: n/a; 2024/25: n/a; 2025/26: 100 per cent; 2026/27: 100 per cent)

The Governments of the Sudan and South Sudan have the primary responsibility to protect civilians and implement the Agreements of: (a) 20 June 2011, on Temporary Arrangements for the Administration and Security of the Abyei Area; (b) 27 September 2012, on Security Arrangements; (c) 30 July 2011, on the Border Monitoring Support Mission; and (d) 29 June 2011, on Border Security and the Joint Political and Security Mechanism through joint mechanisms, including the Abyei Joint Oversight Committee. The African Union and the African Union High-level Implementation Panel play a key role in moving the political process forward, with support from the United Nations. Other enablers include the establishment of the Abyei Police Service by both parties pursuant to the 20 June 2011 Agreement, demilitarization of the Abyei Area from State forces and accordance of full freedom of movement to UNISFA, including commitment and support from the parties and the communities. Implementation of the UNISFA mandate will also be shaped by changing dynamics in areas such as intercommunal and intracommunal fighting, climatic changes, including floods, and expansion of the humanitarian crisis, leading to civilian displacement and casualties. Any political progress and the operational environment will depend on developments in the ongoing conflict in the Sudan.

The Governments of the Sudan and South Sudan have the primary responsibility to protect civilians and implement the Agreements of: (a) 20 June 2011, on Temporary Arrangements for the Administration and Security of the Abyei Area; (b) 27 September 2012, on Security Arrangements; (c) 30 July 2011, on the Border Monitoring Support Mission; and (d) 29 June 2011, on Border Security and the Joint Political and Security Mechanism through joint mechanisms, including the Abyei Joint Oversight Committee. The African Union and the African Union High-level Implementation Panel play a key role in moving the political process forward, with support from the United Nations. Other enablers include the establishment of the Abyei Police Service by both parties pursuant to the 20 June 2011 Agreement, demilitarization of the Abyei Area from State forces and accordance of full freedom of movement to UNISFA, including commitment and support from the parties and the communities. Implementation of the UNISFA mandate will also be shaped by changing dynamics in areas such as intercommunal and intracommunal fighting, climatic changes, including floods, and expansion of the humanitarian crisis, leading to civilian displacement and casualties. Any political progress and the operational environment will depend on developments in the ongoing conflict in the Sudan.

children.text: Evaluation activities
45.	The following evaluation completed by the Office of Internal Oversight Services during the 2024/25 period has guided UNISFA planning for the 2026/27 period:

•	Evaluation of the contribution of strategic communications to fostering public trust in peacekeeping operations: this thematic evaluation included UNISFA among six missions assessed. It highlighted the importance of strategic communications in building trust, managing crises and engaging communities, especially in conflict-prone areas.
46.	In response to the results of the evaluation referenced above, the Force will:

•	Revise its communications strategy to align with the strategic communications and public information policy, prioritize two-way engagement and develop protocols for message clearance. This includes efforts to counter misinformation and hate speech, especially in high-risk areas such as Amiet market;

•	Strengthen collaboration between civilian and uniformed components, particularly in protection of civilians and community engagement, aligning with the recommendation in the evaluation for integrated communications and outreach.
47.	No self-evaluations are scheduled for the 2026/27 period.
A.	Overall
(Thousands of United States dollars; budget year is from 1 July to 30 June)
B.	Non-budgeted contributions

C.	Efficiency gains
49.	The cost estimates for the 2026/27 period reflect the following efficiency initiatives:

table_content.text: Investment since inception to generate the efficiency gains of 2026

Reduced deployment of military staff officers

Mounding blocks for hard walls in house; reusing materials from demolitions; motion sensor lighting in buildings; integrating gravity sewage flow

April 2025: new camp management contractor; August 2025: downsizing rented facilities in Port Sudan to space shared 50/50 with IOM

Solar power: installing solar plants in 3 camps; for irrigation, recycling water, reducing fuel use from heating water 
Ecological: producing seedlings in-house using collected seeds; using internally grown trees and/or shrubs as fencing; growing fruit and vegetables on site; trapping, neutering and returning animals; remediating contaminated soil in-house 
Recycling: generating reusable materials from metals, plastic and glass; composting manure in house; waste management yard to enhance collection, segregation and incineration 
Water and wastewater management: reusing treated wastewater for irrigation, car wash and dust suppression; expanding in-house testing of water quality; using elevated tanks for gravity-fed water distribution; repurposing waste heat from generators for use in water heating

2024/25: modifying transport workshop; providing some technical support remotely; acquiring a refurbished fire truck instead of a new one;
2026/27: replacing 25 light passenger vehicles: annual savings for replacement batteries and tyres, fuel use and repairs
Reduction by one helicopter plus fuel costs
Fewer contracted personnel and VMWare Core and Veritas backup licences (using in-house capacity)
Troops operating UNISFA hospital supplying medicines that UNISFA had been purchasing externally

Note: The amounts represent the total obligations that were cancelled at the end of the 12-month period following the end of the budget period in respect of goods supplied and services rendered in the budget period, and at the end of an additional period of four years in respect of obligations to troop- and police-contributing countries.
F.	Other revenue and adjustments, and borrowing
1.	Other revenue and adjustments
(Thousands of United States dollars)

a	Breakdown of other/miscellaneous revenue: refund of prior-period expenses ($77,000); non-spendable revenue for credit return from peacekeeping cost recovery funds reclassified from spendable revenue from prior periods ($0); and other revenue ($94,600).

52.	Credits comprising the unencumbered balances, other revenue and the cancellation of prior-period obligations reduce the assessed contributions owed by Member States in the following financial year. The credit for the 2024/25 period for overall peacekeeping operations amounts to $335.5 million, including the support entities and the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali (MINUSMA). In the current financial situation, offsetting this large amount of credit against a future assessment for the 2026/27 period will reduce the collection and undermine the ability of peacekeeping operations to fully implement their mandates. Accordingly, the Secretary-General proposes that the General Assembly approve that the credits in respect of the financial period 2024/25 be placed in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the implementation of those mandates; and allow Member States to apply their share towards current or future dues once the Assembly determines that the liquidity situation has sufficiently improved. This proposal is explained in more detail in the peacekeeping overview report.
2.	Borrowing
53.	Owing to its cash liquidity situation, the Force received loans from the Peacekeeping Reserve Fund and other active peacekeeping missions with the month-end balance as follows:
(Millions of United States dollars)
Abbreviations: MONUSCO, United Nations Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, UNIFIL, United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon.
G.	Vacancy factors

54.	The cost estimates for the 2026/27 period reflect the vacancy factors outlined in the table below. The table also summarizes, by category, the actual rates in the periods from 2022/23 to 2024/25, as well as the approved and current rates for the 2025/26 period.
(Percentage)
a	Funded under general temporary assistance.

55.	The vacancy factors reflect the high number of abolishments included in the proposed budget for 2026/27. Accordingly, the proposed vacancy rates for civilian personnel categories with abolishments were estimated using the total number of vacant posts and positions as at 31 December 2025, net of the number of posts and positions proposed for abolishment. For categories without proposed abolishments, the standard policy guidance was applied, using either the 12-month average vacancy rate from January to December 2025 or the actual vacancy rate as at 31 December 2025, whichever was lower. For military and police personnel, the vacancy factors reflect their planned deployment during the period. This approach ensures that vacancy rates are based on, to the extent possible, the actual data while maintaining consistency across the proposed budgets. A vacancy rate of 50 per cent has been applied in the calculation of costs for the proposed establishments and reassignments.
H.	Analysis of requirements for air operations

56.	The flight hour utilization rates for the periods from 2022/23 to 2024/25, the approved and proposed number of flight hours for the 2025/26 and 2026/27 periods, respectively, and the number of flight hours calculated reflecting the new policy guidance are as follows:

57.	UNISFA proposes to deploy seven aircraft for the 2026/27 period, comprising two fixed-wing aircraft (1,800 proposed flight hours, unchanged from the approved budget) and five rotary-wing aircraft, comprising one civilian helicopter and four military helicopters under letters of assist (3,324 proposed flight hours, 522 fewer than the approved budget), attributable primarily to the reduction by one civilian helicopter, compared with the 2025/26 period, but the retention of some of its flight hours to maintain operational flying capability for the mission. The proposed flight hours presented above reflect the policy applied during the formulation of the 2026/27 budget, with the aim of optimizing resource allocation based on strategic priorities, operational requirements and planning assumptions for the period.
I.	Programmatic activities
58.	The estimated resource requirements for programmatic activities for the 2026/27 period, compared with previous periods, are as follows:
(Thousands of United States dollars)

table_content.text: Mine detection and mine clearing services
Rule of law and security sector reform

children.text: 59.	Detailed information on programmatic activities undertaken during the 2024/25 period, as well as on activities planned for the 2026/27 period, is provided in the supplementary information. The resource requirements for the performance and budget periods are for projects implemented by the Force to support mandate implementation as outlined in the Force’s results-based budgeting framework.
J.	Quick-impact projects
60.	The estimated resource requirements for quick-impact projects for the 2026/27 period, compared with previous periods, are as follows:
(Thousands of United States dollars)
61.	The proposed resource requirements for the 2026/27 period amount to $0.8 million, unchanged from the approved budget for the 2025/26 period.

62.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to higher costs for mission subsistence allowance, owing to the higher actual daily rate, effective 1 January 2025, as compared with the approved budget; and to a lower actual average vacancy rate of 6.7 per cent, compared with 10.4 per cent in the approved budget.

63.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the application of a higher vacancy rate of 44.4 per cent, compared with the rate of 7.4 per cent in the approved budget.

64.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) a retroactive increase in the contractual rate for rations, owing to the need to use the southern main supply route because of the ongoing conflict in the Sudan; (b) the higher cost of commercial flights for troop rotations; (c) additional demurrage and storage charges for contingent-owned equipment owing to its delayed release by the authorities and to the continuing conflict in the Sudan; (d) higher costs for mission subsistence allowance, owing to the higher actual daily rate, effective 1 January 2025; and (e) a lower actual average vacancy rate of 0.1 per cent, compared with 2.9 per cent in the approved budget.

65.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the application of a higher vacancy rate of 12.6 per cent, compared with the rate of 0.2 per cent in the approved budget.

66.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) higher costs for mission subsistence allowance, owing to the higher actual daily rate, effective 1 January 2025, as compared with the approved budget; and (b) a lower actual average vacancy rate of 62.2 per cent, compared with 70.3 per cent in the approved budget.

67.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to the application of a lower vacancy rate of 61.5 per cent, compared with the rate of 64.9 per cent in the approved budget.

68.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) a higher actual average vacancy rate of 11.6 per cent, compared with 9.6 per cent in the approved budget, owing to a hiring freeze due to liquidity constraints; (b) the lower actual average post adjustment multiplier applied in the computation of international staff salaries in the approved budget; and (c) lower requirements for danger pay owing to a lower-than-budgeted number of days spent by staff in duty stations that were eligible for danger pay.

69.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: the abolishment of a net five international posts, as well as to the application of a higher vacancy rate of 16.8 per cent, compared with the rate of 10.0 per cent in the approved budget.

70.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) the full incumbency for the authorized complement of 10 national officers as compared with a vacancy rate of 20 per cent in the approved budget; and (b) the higher grade and step levels of the incumbents, as compared with the approved budget.

71.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) higher-than-budgeted grade and step levels of the incumbents; and (b) the payment of unbudgeted relocation allowances for national General Service staff relocated from Khartoum and Kadugli.

72.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the application of the higher vacancy rate of 21.0 per cent, compared with the rate of 6.0 per cent in the approved budget.

73.	This is offset in part by the proposed establishment of 17 national General Service posts, for which the requirements were previously provided for under general temporary assistance: other salaries.
74.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to lower-than-budgeted entitlements, as compared with the approved budget.
75.	This was offset in part by a lower actual average vacancy rate of 2.6 per cent, compared with 13.2 per cent in the approved budget.

76.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to the proposed establishment of two international and two national United Nations Volunteer positions, all proposed to be established with a vacancy rate of 50 per cent.

77.	This is offset in part by the application of higher vacancy rates of 5.3 per cent for international and 100 per cent for national United Nations Volunteer positions, compared with 2.6 per cent for international United Nations Volunteer positions and 50 per cent for 1 newly approved national United Nations Volunteer position in the approved budget.

78.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to the full incumbency for nine mine action-related positions which had been approved for the 2024/25 period at a vacancy rate of 50.0 per cent in respect of new positions.

79.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to (a) removal of the provision for 37 general temporary assistance replacement positions; (b) the proposed abolishment of one international temporary position (P-3); and (c) application of the higher vacancy rate of 16.7 per cent for international general temporary assistance positions, compared with 0.0 per cent in the approved budget.

80.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to cancellation and suspension of planned official travel and approximately 60 per cent of planned training travel as part of the measures implemented by UNISFA owing to the liquidity constraints.

81.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) construction, alteration and major maintenance; and (b) acquisition of construction materials and field defence supplies. Both were affected by deliberate measures to scale down and/or defer construction and major maintenance projects and related acquisitions, owing to liquidity constraints, and to the need for UNISFA to reprioritize resources to meet increased requirements for uniformed personnel.

82.	This was offset in part by: (a) the higher actual consumption of fuel at a higher actual average price per litre, compared with budgeted estimates; and (b) unbudgeted maintenance services costs, including mobilization and demobilization costs related to a change in April 2025 to a new, lower-cost, camp management services contractor and to installation costs for prefabricated units in Athony, Agok and Highway camps.

83.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to construction, alteration, renovation and major maintenance and to maintenance services, relating to labour and other costs of camp construction projects in Goli, Noong and Marial Achak and of the construction of the Wau logistics base.
84.	This is offset in part by the lower estimated average price per litre of fuel for generators of $1.519, compared with the price of $1.712 in the approved budget.

85.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to sufficient stocks of some spare parts being on hand, and to the need for UNISFA to defer repairs and maintenance and acquisitions of workshop equipment owing to the liquidity constraints and to the need for UNISFA to reprioritize resources to meet the increased requirements for uniformed personnel.
86.	This was offset in part by a higher actual average price of $1.69 per litre for diesel fuel, compared with the budgeted price of $1.51 per litre.

87.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to a new solicitation for transport services to replace the current workshop and driver operator contracts in all sections of UNISFA and to consolidate them under the Transport Unit.

88.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to a lower actual number of flight hours for fixed-wing and rotary-wing aircraft as compared with budgeted estimates, owing mainly to the cancellation of the contract for one fixed-wing aircraft, to the unavailability of rotary-wing aircraft owing to scheduled maintenance and unexpected technical issues and to the ongoing closure of Sudanese airspace owing to the security situation.

89.	This was offset in part by the higher actual consumption of 5.4 million litres of fuel for aviation as compared with the budgeted consumption of 4.7 million litres, at a higher actual average price of $1.30 per litre as compared with the budgeted price of $1.17 per litre.
90.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the proposed reduction by one helicopter and to a decrease of 522 flight hours for helicopters, from 3,846 to 3,324.

91.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to the need for an additional 41 20-foot sea containers to support acquisition of prefabricated accommodation units, 30 refrigerators and 30 electric cookers, all of which required containerized transport as part of the freight arrangements.

92.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to the need to procure sea containers for the acquisition, transportation and ongoing storage of goods, which requirement had been met in the 2023/24 and 2024/25 period by UNISFA being able to procure similar items from MINUSMA.

93.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) new, lower-cost, contractual arrangements and a reduction in the number of contracted personnel for maintenance of communications and information technology; and (b) the interruption and eventual cancellation of local mobile network services owing to the conflict in the Sudan.

94.	This was offset in part by increased requirements for materials and equipment for the new communications hub, for modular technology centres and for camp reconstruction, expansion and conversion projects.

95.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to a reduction from 49 to 20 in the number of communications and information technology contractor personnel for maintenance of equipment and support.

96.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the lower volume of medications needing to be procured as compared with the approved budget, owing primarily to troop-contributing countries arriving with adequate medical supplies, including emergency life-saving drugs which had previously been provided by UNISFA.

97.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the ability of UNISFA to utilize the contingent-owned equipment of the troop-contributing country level I clinic in order to support the UNISFA telemedicine platform at other team sites. This strategic shift has allowed for more efficient use of existing resources while maintaining essential medical support capabilities across the UNISFA area of operations.

98.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) lower freight costs, owing to the liquidity constraints, postponing 27.3 per cent of its planned acquisitions of goods and materials during the 2024/25 period and cancelling transportation from the United Nations Logistics Bases at Brindisi, Italy, and Entebbe to Abyei of 151 containers of ablution units, engineering materials, armoured vehicles and spare parts; and (b) the procurement of specialized mine action services through United Nations Headquarters, whereas the budget for 2024/25 had been prepared on the basis of the cost of the contract with the United Nations Office for Project Services (UNOPS) for these same services for the 2023/24 period.

99.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) freight, owing to an anticipated decrease in the number of acquisitions, which is expected to lessen the scope of requirements for freight forwarding, inland transportation and customs clearance services; and (b) mine detection and mine clearing services, owing to the elimination of two six-person third-party mine action patrol support teams, comprising 12 personnel, which had been supporting Joint Border Verification Monitoring Mechanism activities, but whose functions have been integrated into UNISFA operations in the Abyei Area.

text: IV.	Actions to be taken by the General Assembly

children.text: 100.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly in connection with the financing of the United Nations Interim Security Force for Abyei are:
(a)	To take note of the unencumbered balance of $972,100 with respect to the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025;

(b)	To take note of other revenue for the period ended 30 June 2025 amounting to $3,991,200 from investment revenue $2,041,700, other/miscellaneous revenue $171,600 and the cancellation of prior-period obligations $1,777,900;

(c)	To approve the apportionment of the amount of $4,963,300 among Member States in accordance with the applicable scale of assessment for 2025 and to place such apportioned amount in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the full, efficient and effective implementation of mandates;

(d) 	To allow Member States to apply their share of the amount apportioned in subparagraph (c) above towards current or future dues once the General Assembly determines that the liquidity situation has improved sufficiently to allow such application;
(e)	To appropriate the amount of $277,238,100 for the maintenance of the Force for the 12-month period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027;
(f)	To assess the amount in subparagraph (e) above at a monthly rate of $23,103,175 should the Security Council decide to continue the mandate of the Force.

text: V.	Summary of follow-up action taken to implement the decisions and requests of the General Assembly in its resolutions 76/274 and 79/301, including the requests and recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions endorsed by the Assembly

children.text: A.	General Assembly
Cross-cutting issues
(Resolution 76/274)

table_content.text: Requests the Secretary-General to improve comprehensive oversight of the activities of peacekeeping missions and implement the recommendations of relevant oversight bodies in this regard to avoid deficiencies in management and related economic losses, with the aim of ensuring full compliance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations (para. 17).

As at 31 December 2025, 69 of the 99 recommendations (70 per cent) issued by the Office of Internal Oversight Services and 49 of the 62 recommendations (79 per cent) issued by the Board of Auditors were implemented. Regular follow-ups are undertaken to ensure the timely closure of the remaining recommendations.

Requests that future budget proposals demonstrate the scalability of mission support components, including their staffing and operational costs, be proportionate in relation to the changing level of other mission components and include standard indicators (para. 27).
The mission support ratio (the total number of mission support and security personnel per 1,000 total mission personnel) in the 2025/26 period is 63.5, higher than 55.7 in the 2020/21 period.

Requests the Secretary-General to consider options for greater nationalization of functions when formulating budget submissions, commensurate with mission mandates and requirements where applicable (para. 34).

The civilian staffing review considered potential avenues for nationalization of positions, considering the limited availability of highly skilled candidates in the local labour pool in Abyei. Also continuing are efforts to ensure capacity-building and specialized trainings through community programmes and third-party partners.

children.text: B.	Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions
Financing of the United Nations Interim Security Force for Abyei
(A/79/724/Add.11 and General Assembly resolution 79/301)

table_content.text: The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information on the status of vacant posts, as well as on the impact of the liquidity situation on projected hirings, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next budget submission. The Committee also trusts that the long-vacant posts will be critically reviewed in the context of the upcoming civilian staffing review (para. 21).

Updated information on the status of vacant posts will be provided in the supplementary information for the present report. The civilian staffing review conducted during the first half of the 2025/26 period reviewed all UNISFA posts and made recommendations on those that are essential for effective mandate implementation. Owing to liquidity constraints, UNISFA is currently subject to a recruitment freeze, which has led to a higher number of vacant posts and positions.

The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information on the financial impact of the supply route change will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next budget submission (para. 26).

The transition from the northern main supply route via Port Sudan to the southern route following the outbreak of conflict in April 2023 has resulted in a significant increase in logistics costs. Annual freight forwarding expenditures increased from an average of $2.3 million to $4.7 million, representing an increase of approximately 200 per cent.

The cost escalation is primarily attributable to the longer distance (approximately 500 km), multi‑country transit requirements, reliance on seasonal and unpaved roads, and heightened security risks along the southern main supply route. As a result, unit transportation costs increased by two to three times, with the cost of transporting a 20‑foot container rising from $5,925 via Port Sudan to approximately $12,660 via Mombasa, and from $3,437.50 (Port Sudan–Abyei) to approximately $11,500 (Mombasa–Abyei).

The Advisory Committee trusts that UNISFA will ensure that projects benefit the local population, as intended, and leave a positive legacy of the Force in the local communities. The Committee also trusts that projects will be implemented in accordance with existing policies and guidelines and that updated information, including on planning, implementation and assessment, will be provided in the context of the next proposed budget report (para. 35).

Quick-impact projects are vital tools for UNISFA to secure the support of the local administration and community members by fulfilling their basic needs and ensuring the effective implementation of the UNISFA mandate. UNISFA conducts regular assessments of all projects through monitoring visits and close interactions with local communities. Current UNISFA projects are predominantly in the fields of health, water, education, rule of law and hygiene and sanitation. Quick-impact projects are well received by the communities and build confidence in their perception of the ability of UNISFA to carry out its mandated activities.
